prompt:
Our job-scheduling service writes multi-megabyte manifests to a shared storage node that speaks a tus-style offset protocol. When a pod is restarted mid-upload we just start over, and on big manifests that means five minutes of wasted work and a fat retry queue spike. I need a resumable-upload client, package resumeup in resumeup.go, that picks up where it left off. The acceptance tests are in resumeup_test.go; `go test -race -timeout 30s ./...` green means done. Don't touch the test file.

Note: go-httpretry owns generic retry mechanics; this seed is specifically the OFFSET RECONCILIATION protocol where the server is the authority on what it has received, and the client must reconcile.

Protocol the storage node speaks:
  HEAD /<upload-id> → 200 with Upload-Offset: N (server's confirmed byte count)
  PATCH /<upload-id> → requires Upload-Offset: N header and Content-Type: application/offset+octet-stream body
                        204: chunk accepted (more to send)
                        200 + Upload-Complete: true: final chunk, upload done
                        409: offset mismatch — client must re-probe before retrying

New(client *http.Client, opts ...Option) *Uploader
  Option values: ChunkSize(n int) sets the PATCH body size in bytes (default 4 MiB).

up.Upload(ctx context.Context, url string, data []byte) error
  1. HEAD url → get server offset. If server offset >= len(data), the upload is already complete: return nil.
  2. PATCH from server offset in chunks of ChunkSize until done.
  3. On a 409 from a PATCH: re-probe (HEAD) to get the server's current offset, then resume from there. The server's offset after a 409 may be AHEAD of where we thought we were (an earlier chunk landed asynchronously), or BEHIND (an incomplete write); either way the server's word is final.
  4. Any non-2xx, non-409 PATCH response is a permanent error: return an error that includes the HTTP status code.
  5. Context cancellation propagates into HTTP requests and surfaces as an error.
  6. Never assume the server's offset makes logical sense relative to previous PATCHes — after a 409 always use the fresh HEAD offset, no local bookkeeping override.
